1: 'It shall come to pass in the latter days that the mountain of the house of YHWH shall be established as the highest of the mountains, and it shall be lifted up above the hills; and peoples shall flow to it.' Micah 4:1-3 is nearly identical to Isaiah 2:2-4 — two prophets who were contemporaries share (or one copies) the same eschatological vision. The question of priority is debated; both passages may draw from a common liturgical tradition. The vision of Zion exalted to cosmic mountain status, attracting all nations, directly reverses Micah 3:12 (Zion plowed as a field).
2: 'Many nations shall come, and say: “Come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH, to the house of the God of Jacob, that he may teach us his ways and that we may walk in his paths.” For out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of YHWH from Jerusalem.' The Gentile pilgrimage to Zion for Torah instruction reverses the missionary situation of Jonah: here the nations come to Israel for YHWH's teaching. The 'law' (torah) going forth from Zion is the governing instruction for the new world order, replacing the Assyrian imperial law that goes forth from Nineveh.
3: 'He shall judge between many peoples, and shall decide disputes for strong nations far away; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore.' The universal disarmament oracle: the instruments of war are recycled into agricultural implements. YHWH as arbiter replaces international war. The oracle's famous reversal in Joel 3:10 ('beat your plowshares into swords') does not contradict Micah — Joel addresses the pre-judgment assembly of enemies, Micah the post-judgment era of peace.
4: 'But they shall sit every man under his vine and under his fig tree, and no one shall make them afraid, for the mouth of YHWH of hosts has spoken.' The vine-and-fig-tree image of peaceful domestic security appears in 1 Kings 4:25 (Solomon's kingdom: 'each man under his vine and his fig tree') and becomes a standard eschatological image (Zechariah 3:10). Micah's choice is pointed: the peasants stripped of their vineyards and fields in Micah 2 will in the end sit securely under their own. The promise answers the crime directly.
5: 'For all the peoples walk each in the name of its god, but we will walk in the name of YHWH our God forever and ever.' The affirmation of Israelite exclusive loyalty to YHWH in the context of admitting that other peoples walk in their own gods' names. This is not theological pluralism — YHWH will judge the nations (Micah 4:3) — but a statement of present reality: each nation currently follows its own deity. Israel's response is commitment: anachnu (we, emphatic) will walk in YHWH's name.
6: 'In that day, declares YHWH, I will assemble the lame and gather those who have been driven away and those whom I have afflicted.' The gathering of the exiles is described with three groups: the lame (hatzolea), the driven-away (hanidachah), and those YHWH himself has afflicted (asher hare'oti). The third group is remarkable: YHWH claims authorship of the suffering that drove the people into exile. This is honest theodicy — suffering is not random but purposeful, even if painful.
7: 'And the lame I will make the remnant, and those who were cast off, a strong nation; and YHWH will reign over them in Mount Zion from this time forth and forevermore.' The paradox of divine reversal: the weak become the remnant, the cast-off become the strong nation. The remnant (she'erit) is a key Micah concept (Micah 2:12; Micah 4:7; Micah 5:7-8; Micah 7:18) — YHWH's preserved core through judgment. Mount Zion, plowed under in Micah 3:12, here becomes the seat of eternal divine kingship.
8: 'And you, O tower of the flock, hill of the daughter of Zion, to you shall it come, the former dominion shall come, kingship for the daughter of Jerusalem.' The 'tower of the flock' (migdal-eder) is a shepherd's watchtower — evoking the Bethlehem imagery of Micah 5 and the Davidic shepherd-king. The 'former dominion' (mamlachah harishonah) refers to the Davidic kingdom at its height under David and Solomon — its restoration is the messianic expectation that Micah 5 will elaborate.
9: 'Now why do you cry aloud? Is there no king in you? Has your counselor perished, that pain seized you like a woman in labor?' The questions address the present crisis (likely the Assyrian advance or the Babylonian exile, depending on redactional history). The mockery — 'is there no king?' — cuts deep: Israel's king had failed to provide the security for which monarchy was instituted (1 Samuel 8:20). The birth-pang image will be the vehicle for the restoration oracle of Micah 5.
10: 'Writhe and groan, O daughter of Zion, like a woman in labor, for now you shall go out from the city and dwell in the open country; you shall go to Babylon. There you shall be rescued; there YHWH will redeem you from the hand of your enemies.' Micah specifically names Babylon — remarkable if this is pre-exilic (Micah's Assyrian context precedes the Babylonian empire). This either represents genuine predictive prophecy or a later supplement. The mention of Babylon in the context of exile and redemption anticipates the Deutero-Isaianic theme of Babylon as the place of exile from which YHWH will redeem.
11: 'Now many nations are assembled against you, saying, “Let her be defiled, and let our eyes gaze upon Zion.”' The nations' assault on Zion and their desire to witness her defilement and defeat. The language of 'defilement' (techenaf) applied to Zion picks up the land-defilement theology of Lev 18 — the nations see this as the moment to witness the humiliation of YHWH's people.
12: 'But they do not know the thoughts of YHWH; they do not understand his plan, that he has gathered them as sheaves to the threshing floor.' The nations' assault on Zion is unwittingly part of YHWH's plan — they are gathered like sheaves for the threshing. The threshing-floor image appears in Isaiah 21:10 and is a standard divine-judgment agricultural metaphor: the nations are the grain; Zion is the threshing floor; YHWH is the thresher.
13: 'Arise and thresh, O daughter of Zion, for I will make your horn iron, and I will make your hoofs bronze; you shall beat in pieces many peoples; and shall devote their gain to YHWH, their wealth to the Lord of the whole earth.' Zion becomes the threshing ox — iron horns and bronze hooves — crushing the nations. The 'devoting' (hacharem) of spoil to YHWH echoes the herem (holy war) tradition of Joshua. The oracle swings from Zion's weakness (Micah 4:9-11) to her invincibility under divine empowerment — the classic rescue-of-the-besieged pattern.
